title: Virtual Memory
content:
T14d4,

Virtual memory is a memory management technique that provides an "idealized abstraction of the storage resources" available on a machine. It creates the illusion of a very large main memory to users, even if physical RAM is limited.

Virtual memory works by using both the computer's physical memory (RAM) and a portion of the storage device (hard disk or SSD). When a program requires more memory than what is physically available, the operating system temporarily moves inactive data to a pre-configured disk space called swap space or page file. This allows the system to run larger applications and multitask more efficiently.

Advantages of Virtual Memory

  • Increases the effective memory capacity.
  • Allows larger applications to run on systems with limited physical RAM.
  • Enables multiple programs to run simultaneously without exhausting physical memory.
  • Provides an abstraction layer between hardware and applications.
  • Improves system stability by preventing interference between applications.
  • Enhances security and reliability; one process’s crash doesn’t affect others.

    • Paging vs Segmentation

    Paging and Segmentation are two approaches to memory management:

  • Paging: Divides virtual memory into fixed-size blocks called pages, and physical memory into frames of the same size. Any page can be loaded into any frame, eliminating external fragmentation but potentially causing internal fragmentation.
  • Segmentation: Divides memory into variable-sized segments based on logical units like functions, arrays, or modules. This matches program structure but may lead to external fragmentation and requires complex memory management.

    • Comparison Table: Paging vs Segmentation

    Feature Paging Segmentation
    Memory Division Fixed-size pages Variable-size segments
    Physical Memory Divided into frames same size as pages Divided into segments of varying sizes
    Address Structure Single-level (page number + offset) Two-level (segment number + offset)
    Fragmentation No external fragmentation, may cause internal fragmentation Can cause external fragmentation, no internal fragmentation
    Logical Division Does not consider program structure Matches logical structure (functions, arrays)
    Ease of Management Simple due to fixed-size pages Complex due to variable-size segments
    Protection and Sharing Easier at page level Intuitive for logical groupings but complex
    Memory Access Uniform and consistent access time Variable access time depending on segment
    Use Case Common in modern OS Used for specific applications requiring logical divisions

    Logical and Physical Address Space

    `

    Logical Address Space: Also known as virtual address space. These addresses are generated by the CPU and are independent of actual physical memory. They provide a continuous, consistent abstraction layer, simplifying programming and enhancing portability.

    Physical Address Space: The actual location in the computer’s RAM. The Memory Management Unit (MMU) uses these addresses to access data stored in physical memory. The physical address space is finite and corresponds directly to installed hardware.

    Virtual memory allows the OS to map the logical address space of each process onto the physical memory, ensuring efficient memory management and process isolation.

    Address Translation

    Address Translation converts logical addresses into physical addresses, handled by the MMU. The process involves:

  • The CPU generates a logical address (page number + offset).
  • The MMU indexes the page table using the page number to find the corresponding frame in physical memory.
  • The frame number is combined with the offset to form the physical address.
  • The physical address is used to access data in RAM.

    • This ensures that each process has its own isolated logical address space, supports paging and segmentation, and enhances memory protection and efficiency.
